full_text:
Optimization of the HGSOC Tumor Tissue Dissociation Protocol

Aim: To optimize the protocol for HGSOC tissue dissociation to obtain most viable tumor and immune cell populations.

Background: Tissue dissociation protocol vary depending on the stiffness, cellularity and nature of the tissue type. Major factors affecting cell viability, stress response and cellular compositions are nature of the tissue dissociating enzymes, temperature and the incubation time (Flanagan et al., 2019, Adam et al., 2017). In addition, buffers and media used in the protocol might also have an effect on the cell viability and the composition. Kopper er al., used collagenase for ovarian cancer tissue dissociation to establish organoid cultures. However, Flanagan et al., show that compared to dissociation with cold active protease at 60C, digestion with collagenase at 370C results in activation of stress response in all the tissue types. Furthermore, cold active protease yielded enhanced capture of T cells and NK cells. Here we test different enzymes, and dissociation temperatures on the HGOC tissue and evaluate the final yield, cell viability and the cell types using immunofluorescence (IF).

Materials:

Laminar hood

Sterile surgical tools and scalpel

Formaldehyde (4% in PBS)

10cm tissue culture plates

Histology cassettes

Collagenase

Dispase

Subtilsin A

AdDF+++ medium: DMEM/F12+ 1x Glutamax+ 10mM HEPES+ Penstrep

RQ1-RNAse free DNAse

DPBS+5mM Cacl2+125U DNase

Rho Kinase Inhibitor (ROCK: Y27632)

RBC lysis buffer

70µm cell strainers

50mL falcon tubes

1.5mL / 2mL Eppendorf tubes

Protocol:

1. Dissection:

1.1. Place the tumor tissue in a 10 cm tissue culture dish inside the laminar hood. Cut the tissue into three pieces (two small and one large piece) using a sterile scalpel and or scissors. Place one of the small piece into a pre-labelled eppendorf tube and snap freeze in liquid nitrogen for DNA/RNA isolation. Place another small piece into a pre-labelled histo cassettes and fix the tissue in 4% formaldehyde (in 1X PBS) overnight at 4 0C. Use the remaining bigger piece for enzymatic dissociation and single cell isolation.

2. Enzymatic dissociation:

2.1. In the laminar hood, place the big tumor piece in a 10 cm tissue culture dish and mince into smaller pieces using a sterile scalpel and or scissors. Add couple of drops of AdDF+++ while mincing so that tissue does not dry out.

2.2. Divide the minced tumor tissue into three tubes and perform the enzymatic digestion as follows

Tube 1: 1 mg/ml Collagenase in AdDF+++ with 5uM Y-27632, incubate at @ 370C for 1 hr while keeping the tubes on a shaker.

Tube 2: 1 U/ml Dispase in AdDF+++ with 5uM Y-27632, incubate at @ 370C for 1 hr while keeping the tubes on a shaker.

Tube 3: 10 mg/ ml Subtilsin A in DPBS+5mM Cacl2+ 5uM Y-27632 @ 60C for 30 min while keeping the tubes on a shaker.

3. Filtration and centrifugation

3.1. Pre-wet 70µm strainer with cold AdDF+++ medium +5uM Y-27632+50U DNase, or DPBS+5mM Cacl2+ 5uM Y-27632+50U DNase. Pipette the digested suspensions through the strainer into 50mL tubes and wash the strainers with 5 mL above cold medium.

3.2. Centrifuge 10 minutes @ 1000 rpm 4oC.

3.3. Repeat the step 2.2. for the tissue fragments retained on top of the cell strainer.

4. Red blood cell lysis (ONLY if there is a visible red pellet)

4.1. Remove supernatant and resuspend the cell pellet in 2 mL RBC lysis buffer, incubate at room temperature (RT) for 5 min. Add 10 ml AdDF+++ Centrifuge 10 minutes @ 1000 rpm 4oC.

5. Resuspension of the cell pellet for downstream analysis and cultivation

5.1. Carefully remove the supernatant and resuspend the cell pellet in AdDF+++ medium. Perform and cell count and determine total cell yield.

5.2. Pipette 5ul of concentrated cell suspension on top of a Poly-D-Lysine hydrobromide (PLD) coated slides. Air-dry horizontally 5-10 min until cells do not move (do not over-dry!) Fix 10min @ 40C with PFA and wash with PBS. Proceed with the IF.

5.3. Determine the cell concentration required for organoid culture and proceed with the cultivation.

NOTE: Refer to a detailed protocol for PLD coating, immunofluorescence, and organoid culture.
